title: Managing aggression in a rabbit colony
subtitle: Identifying the causes of fighting and when to separate.
excerpt: Rabbits in a colony system establish a hierarchy. Most initial tension resolves within a few days. But serious fighting, causing wounds to the face, ears, or genitals, is a sign that the group composition or space is wrong. Intervening early prevents injuries that become infected.
sourceNotes: RSPCA rabbit behaviour guidance, rspca.org.uk. RWAF colony housing advice. British Rabbit Council management notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The social dominance structure within a rabbit group. Established through chasing, mounting, and occasional fighting. Once settled, tension reduces significantly.
The habit of dominant rabbits pulling fur from lower-ranking companions. Typically a sign of stress, overcrowding, or insufficient enrichment rather than aggressive intent.

content.text: A colony of rabbits is not a peaceful system by default. The

is established by conflict, and some level of chasing and mounting is normal when a group is first formed or when a new rabbit is introduced. The question is whether the aggression is resolving itself or escalating.

In the first twenty-four to seventy-two hours after a new group forms, expect: chasing, circling, mounting (including female-on-female and male-on-male), and thumping. Minor fur pulling and some nipping is typical. As long as no blood is drawn and the lower-ranked rabbits can escape to a different area, this is normal social structuring.

Separate the rabbits straight away in these cases. A rabbit has a bleeding wound. Bites are aimed at the face or genitals. A rabbit is cornered with no escape. One animal has been under attack for more than thirty minutes.

technique to remove the injured rabbit without stressing it further. Check the wound for depth; a bite to the ear that merely breaks the skin is minor; a puncture near the eye or a wound exposing tissue at the genitals needs veterinary attention.
Intact males in the group. Even with a colony of does, an intact buck will fight with competitors and mount does to exhaustion. Castrate bucks at eight weeks or keep single-sex groups.
Insufficient space. Recommended minimum for a colony of four is 3 square metres of floor space. Rabbits with nowhere to escape rank-related attention cannot deescalate.
Insufficient hides and bolt-holes. A colony without enough sheltered spots forces low-ranking rabbits into the open. Cardboard boxes, wooden tunnels, and raised platforms all help.
, fur pulling without breaking the skin, is usually a sign of boredom or stress rather than dominance aggression. Increase enrichment: willow branches, cardboard to shred, hay piles.

A rabbit removed from a colony and then returned after even forty-eight hours may be treated as a stranger and attacked again. Reintroduce on neutral ground, a clean pen neither rabbit has been in before. Add both animals simultaneously rather than placing the returning rabbit back into the group's existing territory.
